title: Concertina with sewn-in signature extensions
subtitle: Extending a folded accordion book by sewing additional signatures into the valley folds
excerpt: A plain concertina opens as one flat strip. Sewing small pamphlet signatures into the valley folds of each panel gives each page its own leaflet structure. The result is a hybrid form with both the continuous opening of a concertina and the page-turning depth of a sewn book.
sourceNotes: The concertina accordion is documented in Cockerell's Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901) as an accordion-fold album structure. The signature-extension technique is a widely practised workshop modification combining pamphlet sewing with an accordion base; no single pre-1928 source documents it in this combined form.

glossaryTerms.definition: A fold in which the paper folds toward the viewer, creating a valley shape when viewed from the side. In a concertina, alternate folds are mountain and valley.
A continuous strip of paper folded in alternating mountain and valley folds so the pages unfold to a full flat strip. Also called an accordion fold book.

content.text: A
concertina

book opens to a panorama: every fold face shows simultaneously when the book is extended. The limitation is page count and depth: each concertina panel is one face, and the format does not suit close writing. Adding sewn signatures into the
valley folds
addresses both limits. Each panel gains a leaflet of pages that folds into the valley, adding ten or twelve pages per panel without changing the concertina's panoramic viewing mode.
What you need
Making the accordion base
This example makes an A6 accordion with four panels. Paper grain must run parallel to the fold direction, which is the short dimension of each A6 panel.

Cut a strip of 220 gsm cartridge 158 mm tall and 440 mm long, long-grain with the grain running along the 158 mm dimension (parallel to the folds). If a single piece is too wide, join two pieces with an overlapping butt join using a thin strip of acid-free PVA.
Mark the strip every 110 mm for four panels of equal width.
Score each marked line with the bone folder and ruler. Fold to form the accordion, alternating mountain and valley folds. Press each fold firmly.
Preparing the signature extensions
For each valley fold panel, cut four sheets of 130 gsm cartridge to 105 x 148 mm (A6), long-grain. Fold each to A7 (74 x 105 mm). Nest the four into one signature.
Pierce three sewing stations in each signature fold: one at the centre, one 12 mm from the head, one 12 mm from the tail.
Sewing signatures into the accordion
Open the first valley fold of the accordion and mark three sewing stations in the valley fold line, matching the signature station positions.
Pierce the stations through the accordion valley fold with the bookbinding awl.
Nest the signature inside the valley fold so the fold of the signature sits in the valley fold of the accordion. Align the station holes.

Sew the pamphlet stitch. Enter the centre hole from outside. Exit at the head. Re-enter the head. Exit at centre, beside the long thread. Continue to the tail hole. Exit, then re-enter the tail. Return to centre and tie off.
Repeat for each valley fold panel.
Finishing

The completed book can be cased into boards attached to the first and last accordion panels, or left with a soft cover wrapped around the outside. Press under weights for two hours after any gluing stage.

items.symptom: Signatures do not sit flush in the valley folds
items.cause: The signature is slightly wider than the accordion panel.
items.fix: Trim the signature foredge by 1 to 2 mm with a craft knife and ruler after sewing.

items.symptom: The accordion does not close flat when loaded with signatures
items.cause: The signatures are too many sheets or too heavy a paper.
items.fix: Reduce to three sheets per signature (six pages) or use 100 gsm paper instead of 130 gsm.
